Apprentice.

“Still developing — the habits aren't compounding yet. Your thinnest axis is breadth — that's where the next gain is.”

4 reasoning blocks before the diffs, 0 subagents dispatched, and 4 errors recovered from along the way.

Share:Post on X

Your scorecard

Counts are measured; the three scores are a read on your style, grounded in gstack — how you work, not a ranking of how good you are. Steering isn't scored: how hands-on you run agents has no better or worse end, so it's described, not graded. This scorecard grades how you build (gstack); the Agentic Quotient further down grades how you operate agents.
⚠ Limited data. Just 8 sessions and 24 tool calls here — not enough to read your habits with confidence, so these scores lean toward the middle. Run more and check back.
Execution0.0How much you ship, and how fast — your committed-code rate, how much of what you generate actually lands in git, and how hard you delegate to agents.
Planning4.6How much you think before you build — exploring before writing, reasoning depth, and laying out a plan first. (Prompt length was dropped — terse expert prompts shouldn't score below verbose ones.)
Engineering4.7How clean your work is — getting files right early, not re-editing the same one over and over, low error rate, and checking your work.
SteeringShort leash — you stay close and course-correct often~2 actions per turn before you weigh in · the agent checked in on 9% of your prompts

Agentic Quotient — how you operate agents

The scorecard above grades how you BUILD (gstack). The Agentic Quotient grades how you OPERATE AGENTS — orchestration, craft, efficiency, and savvy. A custom metric (not part of paxel). MCP-vs-CLI and tool diversity are described, not graded, like Steering.
33Apprentice
Breadth14/ 30 weight
Orchestration4/33
Skill fluency4/22
Tool command (MCP + CLI)2/28
Discipline4/17
Craft28/ 35 weight
Verification1/40
Grounding20/30
Compounding7/30
Efficiency40/ 20 weight
Steering leverage22/50
Recovery18/50
Savvy75/ 15 weight
Model mix50/50
Token economy25/50
MCP vs CLI — described, not graded
CLI · 7 · 3 toolsMCP · 1 · 1

Ratio 7.0:1 CLI-first. CLI is token-cheap and scriptable — you reach for it on repeatable work and reserve MCP for what CLI can't do (browser, design canvas, device control). Right instinct, not a gap.

Tool diversity · 7 distinct tools, entropy 0.855 — high range available, concentrated use. Not penalized.

Your trajectory

Month by month. When plan limits cap any single month, the slope is the honest signal — not the lifetime totals.

2025-063 calls · 3 prompts · 1d · OC Model X
2026-014 calls · 2 prompts · 2d
2026-0613 calls · 6 prompts · 4d · Opus 4.8

Your growth edge

A few habits to try — each pulled from your own data, not a generic checklist. The /commands in parentheses are optional tools from gstack if you'd rather automate one of them.

Widen the toolbelt

Wire your daily services into the agent

Breadth · Tool command (MCP + CLI) is your thinnest AQ signal. 1 MCP servers and 3 CLIs in evidence. Connect the things you touch every day — issue tracker, browser, cloud — as MCP servers or CLIs, so the agent reaches them directly instead of through you.

Multiply yourself

Run agents in parallel, not in series

Breadth · Orchestration is your thinnest AQ signal. 0 distinct subagent types with a median fan-out of 0. When a task splits into independent pieces, hand them to parallel subagents in one orchestrating session instead of grinding through them serially.

Plan first

Spend more time in Think + Plan

Planning is 4.6. Sketch the plan and reframe the ask before writing code — it's the cheapest place to catch a wrong turn. (gstack front-loads this with /office-hours + /autoplan.)

What we noticed

How much did you ship?

Depends how you count

Edit/Write touched 19 lines and the shell ~0 more — but only 0 actually landed in committed git history. That committed number is the honest one.

How hard do you grind?

2× on one file

Your deepest single-file grind in one session — and 0 files went past 15 edits. Your typical file, though? About 1.2.

How often do things break?

4 errors, 25% recovered

Roughly 16.7 per 100 tool calls — and you kept going after almost all of them.

Which model do you reach for?

Opus 4.8 → PI 1 Model

Opus 4.8 50%, PI 1 Model 33% of turns.

When do you do your best work?

Morning person

You do your heaviest work around 11am.

Do you take weekends off?

Weekday warrior

Your busiest day is Monday; weekends stay quiet.

How long are your prompts?

Consistent length

Median 33 characters, average 32 — pretty steady.

How many agents do you run?

0 subagents

About 0.0 per session, plus 0 background tasks and 0 scheduled runs.

How do you see your agent?

Like a teammate

You bounce ideas off it and ask for pushback — more collaborator than command line.

How polite are you to it?

You say thanks a lot

You said please or thank-you in 4 of your 11 prompts (36%). When the robots take over, they'll remember.

What's your longest run?

3m

Your longest unbroken stretch of active work in a single session.

What's your go-to tool?

Bash

8 calls — more than any other tool.

In your own words

Pulled verbatim from your real prompts (filtered for secrets & PII) — hit ↻ another to reroll. Your go-to and off-the-cuff lines also land on the shareable image; the off-the-cuff one uses whichever you’ve rerolled to, so land on one you like before you download. Everything else stays on this local page, on your machine.

Your biggest crash-out?

“why is the import still failing?? ugh”

One of your most heated prompts. We’ve all been there.

Off the cuff?

“great, now commit it”

One of your more unfiltered asks — straight from the keyboard, unedited.

🔒 Generated entirely on-device by paxel.py — the same analysis Paxel runs, with zero data sent anywhere. Counts measured from your transcripts; archetype & scores are a rubric. Raw metrics in stats.json.
Built by Max Schilling, Roadmap
